text: hello and welcome back to the deep dive it is wonderful to have you with us today it really
is no if you're a regular listener you know our usual style we take a huge stack of articles
books research and we uh we try to cram it all into one conversation we surf across the surface
a lot of topics very fast exactly but today today we're doing something completely different that's
right today we are not surfing we are you could say dropping the anchor dropping the anchor i
like that we're going to stay in one place and we're going to go deep really deep we're focusing
on a single story from a single chapter in a very very old book we are opening up to samuel chapter
13. now this is a story from the bible and before you think oh okay this is a sermon or this is
going to be a history lecture just pause please do because we're doing something very specific today
yeah we're looking at this story with a particular person in mind someone we call
the learner right we know many of you are here to learn english
you want to engage with big ideas with deep psychology with literary analysis but you want
it explained in language that's clear accessible you want the vocabulary broken down you want the
complex ideas taken slowly and that is our mission for today we are going to speak clearly
and we're going to take our time and to help us do that we're not just you know reading the
verses out loud we have a guide a kind of map for this deep dive it's a fantastic book called
the art of biblical narrative by robert alter robert alter is uh just a brilliant scholar
and his whole approach is to look at the bible not just as a religious text but as a masterpiece of
literature right he argues the writers were artists they were incredible artists they use
these very subtle techniques things like silence repetition how a character speaks to tell you
things about the story that aren't spelled out it makes reading it feel like you're a detective
that's a perfect analogy you have to look for the clues read what's in the uh the white space
between the lines precisely so today we are literary detectives and the case we're investigating
is a tragedy the tragedy of 2 samuel chapter 13. okay so let's set the scene for everyone what's
the 30-second summary of this chapter well we're in the royal family of israel this is the family
of the famous king david the david from david and goliath the very same he's a warrior a poet
a national hero but in this chapter we see that his family is well it's a complete mess
a mess is a good word let's use a word for the learner dysfunction
dysfunctional dysfunctional is perfect it means a system like a family that is broken it doesn't
work the way it's supposed to and this family is very broken very the chapter is about a crime
a prince falls in love with his sister he gets some terrible advice he commits a violent act
and that single act sets off this chain reaction of silence and eventually
revenge it honestly sounds like a modern prestige tv drama it is timeless it's about
lust manipulation betrayal and hatred all the big messy human emotions
so are you ready to open the case file i'm ready let's start with section one the setup we need to
meet the cast of characters who do we meet first the first person we meet is amnon amnon okay and
who is he amnon is king david's oldest son now you have to understand in a monarchy a system
ruled by a king being the oldest son is everything he's the crown prince the heir
exactly he is next in line for the throne he has grown up with immense privilege and
power he's used to getting whatever he wants so character one amnon the entitled prince who's next
next is tamar tamar is also a child of king david the text gives us one key detail about her
she is beautiful and she's amnon's sister his half-sister this is an important detail they
have the same father king david but different mothers okay so they didn't grow up in the same
uh nuclear family unit right but she is absolutely his sister and there's a third sibling who is
critical to this story absalom
yes absalom and absalom is tamar's full brother they share the same father and the same mother
which means he would feel a special connection to her a special duty to protect her a very special
duty so we have the entitled prince amnon the beautiful sister kamar and the protective brother
absalom but there's a fourth person in this initial setup the guy kind of standing in the
shadows pulling the strings ah yes jonadab jonadab it's not a name that inspires a lot of
trust is it it's not jonadab is amnon's friend he's also his cousin so he's part of the royal
family and the bible gives him this very specific very interesting label it calls him a very wise
man it does let's stop right there because for the learner the word wise almost always has a good
meaning if i say my grandmother is wise i mean she has experience good judgment she's a moral person
that is the common english definition yes yeah but this is where robert alter's work is so helpful
and i think it's important to remember that the word wise is the original hebrew word which is
haqqam haqqam and in the bible haqqam doesn't always mean morally good sometimes it just means
skilled or clever yeah or even crafty so you could be a wise carpenter meaning you're skilled
with wood exactly or in jonadab's case you can be wise in the art of manipulation he's smart
he's clever he understands human psychology but he's not good he's not good at all he's a technician
he knows how to get a result but he doesn't seem to care one bit if the result is evil that is a
very dangerous combination yeah smart but amoral the most dangerous so jonadab comes onto the scene
because he sees his friend prince amnon has a problem what's wrong with amnon the text says
amnon is making himself ill some translations say he's lovesick he's getting thinner every day he
looks awful he's wasting away because he's in love with his sister tamar yes he desires her but the
narrator adds to that
adds this one crucial detail it says it seemed impossible for amnon to do anything to her
because she was a virgin okay let's unpack that for the learner yeah why does her being
a virgin make it impossible for him to even see her well in this culture at this time the
virgin daughters of the king were heavily guarded they were incredibly valuable assets for political
marriages they lived in a separate part of the palace the women's quarters so she's not just
you know hanging out in the throne room not at all there are physical and social barriers
amnon is the crown prince he can have almost anything he wants in the entire kingdom
but he can't have access to her and that denial that one thing he can't have
it turns his desire into an obsession it absolutely does he wants the one thing he's been
told is forbidden so jonadab the wise friend sees him looking terrible day after day he asks him why
do you the king's son look so downcast morning after morning and amnon confesses he tells him his
secret he says i love tamar my brother absalom's sister oh that's an interesting detail he doesn't
say my sister he doesn't he says my brother absalom's sister it's a subtle but powerful
choice of words he's already trying to create psychological distance he's framing her as
belonging to someone else not as his own kin that's a great catch so jonadab hears this
confession now a good friend a truly wise friend might say whoa amnon stop right there this is a
disaster waiting to happen she's your sister a good friend would but jonadab doesn't see a moral
crisis he sees a puzzle a logistical problem to be solved and he comes up with a plan
a trap an incredibly devious one he tells amnon go to bed lie down pretend you're sick
it sounds so childish like something a kid would do to get out of school
it's simple but it's brilliant in its manipulation of the system jonadab knows the palace is the palace
protocol if the crown prince the heir to the throne is sick the king has to come visit him
the king must come jonadab is using king david the most powerful man in the country as an unwitting
pawn in his scheme he uses the father to get to the daughter precisely and he coaches amnon
on the exact words to use he says when your father comes to see you say to him please let
my sister tamar come and give me bread to eat but the request is more specific than that and it's
it's creepy he doesn't just say bring me food no he doesn't the line is let her prepare
the food in my sight so that i may see it and eat from her hand in my sight he wants to watch her
it's voyeuristic he wants to take this normal domestic act of cooking
and turn it into a private performance for his own pleasure he wants her in his
room isolated working for him while he just lies there and watches and david
king david does he have any clue what's going on this is one of the first great tragedies
of the chapter david seems completely oblivious he's a brilliant military strategist but he can't
see the battle lines being drawn in his own house he just agrees he agrees immediately he sends a
message to tamar and the message is go to your brother amnon's house and prepare food for him
so the father without knowing it sends the lamb into the wolf's den he does and tamar
being a good obedient daughter obeys her father the king she goes to amnon's house which brings
us to section two the encounter and this is where the tension just it goes through the roof you can
imagine the scene tamar arrives at amnon's house he's in bed putting on this act of being sick
the narrative describes her actions so precisely she takes the dough she needs it right and for
the learner kneading is when you push and fold the dough with your hands it's a very physical
hands-on process and the text makes sure to add that she does this before his eyes he's watching
her every move watching her body work she makes these little cakes she bakes them now i've heard
of cakes that are made heart-shaped cakes is there any truth to that that's a fascinating bit of
literary analysis the hebrew word for these cakes is levivot it sounds very very similar to the
hebrew word for heart which is lev so it's a pun a play on words robert alter and many other
scholars believe so it adds this layer of cruel irony she is innocently making these heart cakes
for a man who is all lust and no heart that's incredible these ancient writers were so
sophisticated okay so the cakes are baked
she brings the pan over to him to serve him and then what he refuses to eat the act is over the
charade is done this is the turning point he commands have everyone go out for me he clears
the room all the servants the guards everyone who could possibly witness or help her they're
gone and now they're completely alone he tells her bring the food into the chamber the inner
bedroom so that i may eat from your hand she walks deeper into the trap he grabs her and this right
here is where robert alter points out something just mind-blowing about the way this story is
written this is a real deep dive moment it is alter talks about a literary device called illusion
let's define that for the learner that's a-l-l-u-s-i-o-n
illusion an illusion is when a writer deliberately references an older story that they expect the
reader to know it's like a literary echo it connects what's happening now to something
that happened before to add a layer of meaning so what's the illusion here what's the echo when amnon
grabs tamar he says a very specific phrase in hebrew he says come lie with me my sister come
lie with me okay where have we heard that before in the bible those exact words are used in the
book of genesis in the story of joseph joseph and the coat of many colors when he was a slave
in egypt that's the one when joseph was a slave his master's wife a woman named potiphar's wife
tried to seduce him she was the villain of that story
she grabbed joseph by his robe and commanded him with those exact words lie with me wow
so the biblical writer is putting the words of a famous female villain into amnon's mouth exactly
this is alter's genius insight by using that exact same phrase the narrator is screaming at
us don't you dare think this is a romance this is not a lovesick boy this is a predator he's
acting just like the wicked wife of potiphar that is unbelievably powerful it completely
reframes how you read that line but alter notes a difference right amnon adds one word that
potiphar's wife didn't use he has one devastating word sister come lie with me my sister oh it just
makes it so much worse it's a complete perversion he uses a word of family of kinship of protection
sister at the precise moment he is about to violate that very bond over here he's twisting
the language of love to commit an act of violence it's disgusting now let's look at tamar's response
in the joseph story joseph just runs away he doesn't say a word but tamar tamar speaks she does
and you could argue that in this dark awful story tamar just runs away he doesn't say a word but tamar speaks she does and you could argue that in this
whole chapter tamar is the strongest most heroic character she is physically trapped she's
overpowered but she does not panic she reasons with him she uses logic robert alter says dialogue
reveals character what does tamar's dialogue tell us about her it reveals she is incredibly
intelligent she's moral and she understands her society and its laws she gives him like
four distinct arguments against what he's about to do okay let's listen what's the first one
first she names the act for what it is she says no
my brother do not force me she calls it force not love not passion force exactly then her second
argument for no such thing should be done in israel that's an appeal to their shared culture
their national identity right she's saying we're not barbarians we are the people of god we have
laws this is beneath us then she pivots and warns him about his own reputation she does
she says and as for you you will be as one of the fools in israel now fool here
that's a strong word isn't it yeah it's not just like calling someone silly oh it's much stronger
in the wisdom literature of the bible a fool the hebrew is nabal is someone who is morally
bankrupt an outcast a despicable person she's telling him if you do this you will destroy your
own honor you will lose everything and finally she speaks for herself about what will happen to her
she says and i where could i carry my shame she knows that for a woman in that world this act
is her future she can't make a royal marriage she is in a social sense ruined she's begging him to
see her as a person she even tries one last desperate tactic she suggests a compromise
she does she says please speak to the king for he will not withhold me from you now hold on
would the king really have allowed a brother and sister to marry that was against the law
wasn't it it was absolutely against the laws written in the book of leviticus
most scholars agree david would never ever have permitted it so why does she say it she's
stalling it's a brilliant desperate attempt to buy time she's thinking if i can just get him to stop
to pause to go talk to our father maybe i can get out of this room is her last hope but amnon
he doesn't listen to a single word the text is so blunt it's brutal
but he would not listen to her voice all her logic her pleading her intelligence
it means nothing to him and then the line and since he was stronger than she
was he would not listen to her voice all her logic her pleading her intelligence
it means nothing to him and then the line and since he was stronger than she
was he would not listen to her voice all her logic her pleading her intelligence
because what happens in the instant after this act is one of the most
psychologically astute moments in the entire bible the text says then amnon hated her with
a very great hatred it's even more emphatic than that it says the hatred with which he
hated her was greater than the love with which he had loved her it's such a famous line
one minute he's supposedly sick with love and the next he's filled with this intense
loathing how how do you explain that switch well it proves what his love really was it was never
love it was pure lust an appetite think of it like someone who is starving and obsesses over a meal
the fantasy of the food is everything but once they've gorged themselves the sight of the dirty
plate is disgusting so tamar was just an object to be consumed and once he got what he wanted
he saw her as trash worse than trash she's now a living breathing reminder of his own crime when he
sees her he sees his sin he feels shame but he's a narcissist he can't bear that shame himself so
he projects it onto her he blames her for his own disgusting act he makes her the enemy and he tells
her to get out get up go away and tamar even after everything she argues with him again she
says no sending me away now is even worse help learn understand that how could sending her away
possibly be worse than the rape itself she says this evil in sending me away is greater than the
rape itself she says this evil in sending me away is greater than the rape itself she says this evil in
